coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence enhancement of single-layer graphene quantum dots by the surface plasmon resonance of Au nanocubes
coredata.dc:creator: Dejpasand, Mohamad Taghi
Saievar-Iranizad, Esmaiel
Bayat, Amir
coredata.dc:description: Highly photoluminescent single-layer graphene quantum dots around Au nanocubes (SLGQDs/Au) were synthesized using a simple method. The SLGQDs were synthesized using a low-cost hydrothermal method from glucose as a precursor. The atomic force microscopy analysis proved that the average thickness of the GQDs is lower than 4 Å, meaning that the GQDs are single-layer dots. The novel cubic shape of the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposites was confirmed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy and Transmission electron microscopy. Structural characterization was performed by X-ray diffraction, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy revealed that the obtained SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite has a higher PL emission peak intensity than that of the SLGQDs, which is due to the surface plasmon resonance of Au nanocubes at 530 nm, revealed in the UV–Vis absorption spectra. Finally, the effect of Au concentration on the PL emission peak intensity of the SLGQDs was studied, and the quantum yield of the optimum sample was measured and discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single-layer graphene quantum dot
Gold nanocube
Photoluminescence
Photo-chemical synthesis
Surface plasmon resonance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation mechanism of SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite under UV illumination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of the procedures for synthesis of SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au nanocubes.
(a) An AFM micrograph and (b) a height profile of the synthesized SLGQDs.
(a) and (b) TEM images and (c) a FESEM image of the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposites.
Raman spectra of the bare SLGQDs and the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite.
(a) The EDX profile and (b) the XRD pattern of the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (b) PL spectra of the bare SLGQDs and the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposites and (c) Normalized intensity of PL peaks.
Significant reports on photoluminescence properties of composites of noble metals and Graphene quantum dots.
Quantum yields of the bare SLGQDs and the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence enhancement of single-layer graphene quantum dots by the surface plasmon resonance of Au nanocubes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly photoluminescent single-layer graphene quantum dots around Au nanocubes (SLGQDs/Au) were synthesized using a simple method. The SLGQDs were synthesized using a low-cost hydrothermal method from glucose as a precursor. The atomic force microscopy analysis proved that the average thickness of the GQDs is lower than 4 Å, meaning that the GQDs are single-layer dots. The novel cubic shape of the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposites was confirmed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy and Transmission electron microscopy. Structural characterization was performed by X-ray diffraction, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy revealed that the obtained SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite has a higher PL emission peak intensity than that of the SLGQDs, which is due to the surface plasmon resonance of Au nanocubes at 530 nm, revealed in the UV–Vis absorption spectra. Finally, the effect of Au concentration on the PL emission peak intensity of the SLGQDs was studied, and the quantum yield of the optimum sample was measured and discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted a great deal of attention because of their low toxicity, high physical and chemical stability, high biocompatibility, large surface to mass ratio and low cost [1–5]. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a zero-dimensional carbon structure, have numerous potential applications in various fields like solar cells, bioimaging, drug delivery, water splitting, wastewater treatment, electronic displays, and light emitting diodes [6–13]. Optical properties of GQDs play an important role in most of these applications. The optical characteristics of GQDs such as their absorption, photoemission, energy bandgap, and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels are the focus of recent researches [9,14,15]. Enhancing the emission peak intensity of GQDs can improve their performance in optoelectronic and sensing devices, because a higher photoluminescence peak intensity can minimize the effect of different noises on the efficiency of such devices, resulting in a higher sensitivity.

To enhance the photoluminescence of fluorescent materials, nanoparticles of noble metals like platinum, palladium, silver, and gold have been used due to their special visible light absorption and nanoscale size, which is smaller than the wavelength of the incident visible light. The collective oscillation of high-concentration free electrons generated by the irradiation of light at the surface of the metallic nanoparticles, known as surface plasmon resonance (SPR), can be utilized to enhance the optical properties of other fluorophores such as GQDs. The energy transfer from the surface of the metals by the SPR property can significantly improve the optical characteristics of their adjacent materials [16,17]. There are several reports in this field, some of which are listed in Table 1 .

GQDs are chosen due to their higher surface-to-volume ratio and besides, because of our synthesis method, GQDs seem to be the most suitable option for combination with gold due to their lower reduction potential [24]. On the other hand, gold nanoparticles are chosen over other noble metallic ones due to their low oxidation, high stability, and very good biocompatibility [25]. Despite the high initial cost of gold, it usually yields better results for industrial applications [26–28]. Nevertheless, the low cost of GQDs can offset the high cost of gold nanoparticles.

Here, we synthesized a novel morphology of single-layer GQDs (SLGQDs) and Au nanocubes with the simultaneous reduction of GQDs and organization of Au nanocubes by a simple photochemical procedure. The SLGQD/Au nanocomposites were synthesized by self-assembly via aurophilic intraction and aggregation by hydrogen bonding interactions [29,30]. The light yellow color of the SLGQD solution changed to purple after the formation of SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite. To the best of our knowledge, the nanocube morphology of the SLGQD/Au nanocomposite has been obtained for the first time in this work. The low cost and the simple synthesis of SLGQDs can offset the high price of HAuCl4 as the precursor for Au nanocubes. We observed that the SLGQD/Au nanocomposite show a higher PL intensity and a wider range of emission wavelengths than those of the bare SLGQDs, which is due to the SPR property of Au nanocubes.
All materials and chemical reagents were purchased from Merck Co. (Germany) without any further purification.

For the hydrothermal synthesis of SLGQDs, 0.03 g glucose was stirred in 30 ml deionized water for 15 min at room temperature. The obtained solution was put into a 40-ml stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After that, the colorless solution was changed to a light yellow one. The resulting sample was dialyzed through a dialysis bag with the molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da to remove the oversized particles. A schematic illustration of the procedure is shown in Fig. 1 .

In order to achieve the hybridization of SLGQDs and Au nanocubes, a 3.28 mM solution of HAuCl4 was added into the SLGQDs solution. The SLGQD/Au nanocubes were prepared with a fixed amount SLGQD particles (1 mL) but different amounts of the HAuCl4 solution (50–300 μL) and accordingly the samples were named as SLGQDs/Au50, SLGQDs/Au100, SLGQDs/Au200, and SLGQDs/Au300. Then, by irradiating UV light generated by a 16 W lamp onto the mixture for 2 h, the SLGQD/Au nanocomposite was prepared via a photochemical reaction [19] (Fig. 1). The irradiated UV photons will excite the electrons in the SLGQDs and dissociate HAuCl4 into Au3+, H+, and Cl− ions. The homogenous formation of the SLGQD/Au nanocubes is due to the higher reduction potential of Au3+ than that of the GQDs. The SLGQD/Au nanocomposite were formed via aurophilic and hydrogen bonding interactions by self-assembly and aggregation processes. The pH of the solution can also have a significant role in the formation of the nanocubes [31]. The optimal pH for this composite was found about 5–6. Since the focus of the article is on optical properties, the analyses related to the optically optimized sample (SLGQDs/Au300) are presented in the following.

The energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was carried out by a MIRA3 TESCAN instrument. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) micrographs were taken by a Veeco Autoprobe CP research microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using a CM30 300 kV instrument. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a Philips X'pert instrument with Cu kα radiation. UV–Vis spectra were recorded by a PerkinElmer Lambda 25 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a Gilden photonics PL spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded by a Takram P50C0R10 Raman spectrometer with a 532 nm excitation source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism of SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite under UV illumination
ce:section.ce:para: The UV irradiation with an appropriate frequency can overcome the energy of bonds in the Gold salt HAuCl4 and dissociate it into H+, Cl−, and Au3+, as well as excite the electrons in GQDs. The excited electrons can attach to Au3+ to form Au nanoparticles. Next, the Au nanoparticles are aggregated by the aurophilic interactions. Finally, GQDs/Au nanocomposite are formed by the formation of hydrogen bonds between the GQDs around Au nanoparticles. The pH was found to play a significant role in the morphology of the composite. The optimal pH of the synthesized nanocubes was found about 5–6. The synthesis procedure of the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite is schematically shown in Fig. 1.

AFM analysis was used to estimate the thickness of the obtained SLGQDs. Fig. 2 (a) and (b) show an AFM micrograph and a height profile of the synthesized SLGQDs on a mica substrate with a very smooth surface. The measurement error of the AFM analysis is about 0.1 nm [32]. It is seen that the height of the SLGQDs is less than 0.5 nm, confirming that the synthesized GQDs are single-layer dots.

TEM images of the SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), and a FESEM image of nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 3 (c), where the homogeneous and uniform distribution of the nanoparticles is obvious. Based on the TEM images, the size of the SLGQDs was determined in the range of 7–12 nm with the estimated average size of ~8 nm. Fig. 3 (b) and (c) show nanocomposites with a size in the range of 60–150 nm and an average size of ~65 nm.

Fig. 4 , indicates Raman shift spectra of the synthesized SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au nanocomposites. The Raman modes of the SLGQDs are centered at 1340 and 1615 cm−1, conﬁrming the existence of graphitic sp [2] carbon domains and sp [3] carbon atoms in SLGQDs associated with disordered (D) and crystalline (G) bands, respectively [33,34]. The D and G bands shift to 1404 and 1587 cm−1, respectively, for the SLGQDs/Au sample. This shift in the Raman peaks could be due to increase of electron-phonon coupling arising from charge transfer from GQDs to Au [35], [-37]. The intensity ratio of the disordered peak and the graphitic peak (ID/IG), which represents the degree of disorder and the sp [2] domain size in graphene-based composites [37], was calculated 1.33 and 0.76 for the bare SLGQDs and the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposites, respectively. The decrease in ID/IG indicates that the impurities and disorders in the GQDs are decreased after the hybridization of GQDs and Au. This is due to the participation of free bands and oxygen atoms in the formation of the composite, which could decrease the graphene defects.

The elemental composition of the prepared SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite was analyzed by the EDX analysis, shown in Fig. 5 (a), which indicates the existence of Au, C, and O with their corresponding weight percentages. No other elements were observed in the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite, confirming the high purity of the synthesized composite.

The XRD pattern of the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 5 (b). The weak and broad band between 26 and 30 ̊ can be attributed to the graphitic peak of SLGQDs in the nanocomposite. Two other peaks appear at 44.75 ̊ and 52.15 ̊, which are attributed to the (111) and (200) planes of the face-centered cubic crystalline structure of the Au nanocubes [38]. The XRD pattern of the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite indicates that the crystallinity of the SLGQDs and Au nanocubes has been maintained and a new morphology has been created, which can play a specific role in the improvement of optical properties. The crystalline size of the Au nanocubes, calculated from the Scherrer equation [39], is ~20 nm.

Optical properties were analyzed by UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and PL techniques. Fig. 6 (a) indicates the absorption spectra of colloidal solutions of the bare SLGQDs and the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite. The peak at 241 nm refers to the sp [2] hybridized structure resulting from C–C and CC bonds. The peak at 295 nm is related to the sp [3] hybridized structure resulting from CO bonds due to the n-π* transition [40,41]. The peak appeared at 530 nm in the spectrum of the SLGQDs/Au sample is due to the SPR effect by the existing Au nanocubes.

The position of the SPR absorption peak has a definite relation with the size and shape of gold nanoparticles, which has been mentioned in several articles [42,43]. In this paper, the SPR absorption peak at 530 nm is consistent with the size and shape of gold nanoparticles, which are about 40–50 nm (excluding quantum dot particles).

The PL spectra in Fig. 6 (b) compare the effect of different amounts of the precursor HAuCl4 on the PL peaks' intensity and location of the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite. There are two peaks for the bare SLGQDs at 439 and 464 nm, while the SLGQDs/Au samples show two peaks at about 436 and 470 nm. The displacement of the peaks is due to the larger size of the SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite than the bare SLGQDs and the difference in the number of functional groups combined with the gold nanoparticles. The composition of SLGQDs and Au nanoparticles and the interaction between them assisted by the localized surface plasmon resonance of Au nanoparticles and the extrinsic peak of GQDs has caused the peak at 470 nm [40,44,45].

Luminescence process in this composite is based on the competition between free quantum dots and quantum dots attached to gold nanoparticles. At low concentrations of gold nanoparticles (SLGQDs/Au50 and SLGQDs/Au100), a high portion quantum dots are attached to gold nanoparticles. Therefore, the average distance between attached quantum dots on the gold nanoparticle is not enough to prevent quenching and lead to reduce the intensity of luminescence but free quantum dots maintain their luminescence intensity. However, in a high concentration of gold nanoparticles (SLGQDs/Au200 and SLGQDs/Au300), due to the presence of a sufficient number of gold nanoparticles, the average distance between attached quantum dots on gold nanoparticles is large enough and prevent quenching. On other hand, free quantum dots still have their luminescence intensity. Thus, addition of high amount of gold nanoparticles increases the luminescence intensity.

According to the description provided, Fig. 6 (b) and (c) show that the intensities of the PL peaks almost quench by adding low amounts of HAuCl4 (Au50 and Au100). For low concentrations of Au nanocubes, more GQDs accumulate around each of the gold nanoparticles, and hence smaller distances exist between them, which causes the PL peak intensity to quench. By further increasing the concentration of HauCl4, the number of gold nanoparticles in the sample increases, and the distance between the GQDs increases to an optimal state. Here, the role of the gold nanoparticles as hotspots overcomes the quenching process and we will see an increase in the intensity of the PL peak (Au200 and Au300). Further addition of HAuCl4 will create new bonds between functional groups and Au particles until the mixture of HAuCl4 and the SLGQD dispersion becomes saturated (Au300). Au nanocubes have the role of concentrating the exciting photon energy in a small region, which considerably enhances the local electromagnetic field [46]. The enhancement of these peaks intensity is due to the surface plasmon resonance of Au nanocubes at 530 nm, revealed in the UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 6 (a)). This process reinforces the weak signals to create more excited electrons, which can itself play a significant role in the emission of more photons and the enhancement of the PL peak intensity.

The quantum yield of the bare SLGQDs and the SLGQDs/Au300 nanocomposite (as the optimal sample) was measured by use of quinine sulfate as a reference (shown in Table 2 ). The quantum yield value was calculated according to [9].
ϕ = ϕr (I/Ir)(n2/nr 2)(Ar/A)
where ϕ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index (1.33 for water), and A is the optical density. The subscript “r” refers to the reference material with its known quantum yield. According to Table 2, the PL quantum yields of the bare SLGQDs and the SLGQDs/Au300 were calculated 9% and 22%, respectively. Gold nanoparticles concentrate the energy of the irradiated photons like a nanoantenna and form hotspots, which cause the excitation of more electrons and the formation of more electron-hole pairs. As a result, this process leads to the radiative recombination of more electron-hole pairs, leading to a more intense PL, consistent with the PL spectra, and higher efficiency.

Low-cost single-layer graphene quantum dots (SLGQDs) with a thickness less than 4 Å were synthesized using a hydrothermal method from glucose as the precursor. Then, the composite of the SLGQDs and Au nanocubes site was prepared by a photochemical method. UV–Vis absorption spectra confirm the presence of the gold absorption peak. By examining different concentrations of Au, we determined the optimal concentration to achieve the highest PL peak intensity. The optimal SLGQDs/Au sample yielded a quantum efficiency of more than twice that of the bare SLGQDs.

Mr. MohamadTaghi Dejpasand: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Prof. Esmaiel Saievar-Iranizad: Supervision, Dr. Amir Bayat: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent applications of carbon-based nanomaterials in analytical chemistry: critical review
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ni, Z., Wang, Y., Yu, T. & Shen, Z. Raman spectroscopy and imaging of graphene. Nano Research 1, 273-291 (2008).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-and two-photon luminescence in graphene oxide quantum dots
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, H.-X., Wang, Y.-C., Zhang, L.-Y. & Wang, M. One-and two-photon luminescence in graphene oxide quantum dots. New Journal of Chemistry 39, 98-101 (2015).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: UV irradiation synthesis of an Au–graphene nanocomposite with enhanced electrochemical sensing properties
J. Mater. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, P. et al. UV irradiation synthesis of an Au-graphene nanocomposite with enhanced electrochemical sensing properties. Journal of Materials Chemistry A 1, 9189-9195 (2013).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ controllable growth of noble metal nanodot on graphene sheet
J. Mater. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, H., Chen, S., Quan, X., Yu, H. & Zhao, H. In situ controllable growth of noble metal nanodot on graphene sheet. Journal of Materials Chemistry 21, 12986-12990 (2011).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable wide blue photoluminescence with europium decorated graphene
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Park, B. et al. Tunable wide blue photoluminescence with europium decorated graphene. Journal of materials chemistry C 3, 4030-4038 (2015).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and electron transfer property of sulfhydryl-containing multi-walled carbon nanotube/gold nanoparticle heterojunctions
J. Phys. Appl. Phys.

J. Mater. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Riaz, A. et al. Enhanced intrinsic photocatalytic activity of TiO 2 electrospun nanofibers based on temperature assisted manipulation of crystal phase ratios. Journal of Materials Research 31, 3036-3043 (2016).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blue photoluminescence from chemically derived graphene oxide
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Eda, G. et al. Blue photoluminescence from chemically derived graphene oxide. Advanced materials 22, 505-509 (2010).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blue light emitting graphene-based materials and their use in generating white light
Solid State Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Subrahmanyam, K., Kumar, P., Nag, A. & Rao, C. Blue light emitting graphene-based materials and their use in generating white light. Solid state communications 150, 1774-1777 (2010).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mustafa, D. E. et al. Surface plasmon coupling effect of gold nanoparticles with different shape and size on conventional surface plasmon resonance signal. 5, 221-231 (2010).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Shape and size dependence of the surface plasmon resonance of gold nanoparticles studied by Photoacoustic technique

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: El-Brolossy, T. et al. Shape and size dependence of the surface plasmon resonance of gold nanoparticles studied by Photoacoustic technique. 153, 361-364 (2008).

J. Alloys Compd.

Mater. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xin, G. et al. Tunable photoluminescence of graphene oxide from near-ultraviolet to blue. Materials Letters 74, 71-73 (2012).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hot spots in different metal nanostructures for plasmon-enhanced Raman spectroscopy
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wei, H. & Xu, H. Hot spots in different metal nanostructures for plasmon-enhanced Raman spectroscopy. Nanoscale 5, 10794-10805 (2013).
